response.step_by_step_log: 1. Analyzed video content to identify two distinct musical segments and a transition. 2. Identified the performers in the first segment as Ririka Ichijou, Subaru Oozora, and Hakos Baelz performing 'DIVE!'. 3. Identified the second segment as a solo performance by Raden Juufutei titled 'JAPANの美術史 お・ぼ・え・ま・SHOW!', with songwriting credits for Bocchi Boromaru identified on-screen. 4. Searched for song titles and lyrics. 5. Compiled the scenes and lyrics into the requested JSON structure.
response.global_summary: The video is a recording of a 3D live performance by Hololive VTubers. It begins with a performance of the song 'DIVE!' by Ririka Ichijou, Subaru Oozora, and Hakos Baelz. Following a short transition, the performer Ririka Ichijou delivers a solo performance titled 'JAPANの美術史 お・ぼ・え・ま・SHOW!', which is a high-energy educational rap performance about Japanese art history.

scenes.description: Ririka Ichijou, Subaru Oozora, and Hakos Baelz perform the song 'DIVE!' on stage with choreography.
scenes.performer_in_video: Ririka Ichijou, Subaru Oozora, Hakos Baelz
scenes.reference_lyrics_og: [Lyrics not provided in detail, song identified as 'DIVE!']

scenes.description: Transition scene: stage goes dark, lights move. No vocal music playing.
Ririka Ichijou performs a solo song titled 'JAPANの美術史 お・ぼ・え・ま・SHOW!', which is an educational rap about Japanese art history, including historical periods, artifacts, and figures.
scenes.song_title: JAPANの美術史 お・ぼ・え・ま・SHOW!
scenes.reference_lyrics_og:
JAPANの美術史 (アート！アート！) 
JAPANの美術史 (絵画に立体！) 
JAPANの美術史 (テストもバッチリ！) 
お・ぼ・え・ま・SHOW！ 
[...The song progresses through chronological lists of Japanese art history, mentioning items such as Jomon pottery, Dogu, Dotaku, Haniwa, Buddhist statues, Emakimono scrolls, screen paintings, and more...]
scenes.reference_lyrics_en: JAPAN's Art History (Art! Art!) / JAPAN's Art History (Paintings and Sculptures!) / JAPAN's Art History (Tests are a breeze!) / O-bo-e-ma-SHOW! / [...The song continues with rapid-fire lyrics about Japanese art history periods (Jomon, Yayoi, Asuka, Nara, Heian, Kamakura, Muromachi, Azuchi-Momoyama, Edo, Showa) and specific cultural artifacts...]
